record_office_terminology.land_records: Town Clerk (land evidence records / deeds — each of RI's 39 towns/cities maintains its own land records; counties have no role)
record_office_terminology.probate: Town/City Probate Court (each municipality operates its own probate court; no county-level probate)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Town Clerk (town-level vital registration from colonial era; state registration began 1853; RI Department of Health holds state copies from 1853)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1853 (state copies of town vital records; town-level registration dates to founding of each town, many from the 1630s-1640s); modern vital records through RI Department of Health

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Research Wiki — Rhode Island; Rhode Island State Archives; NEHGS published vital records series; Rhode Island counties have no courthouses (towns hold records), so traditional 'burned county' losses are extremely rare
administrative_notes: Rhode Island was founded in 1636 by Roger Williams as a refuge for religious dissenters from Massachusetts Bay Colony. Originally known as Rhode Island and Providence Plantations (the official name until 2020). One of the 13 original colonies and the last to ratify the US Constitution (May 29, 1790). Rhode Island is unique among US states in that counties have NO governmental functions — they exist only as geographic/judicial districts and FIPS entities. All local government is conducted at the town or city level. There are 39 municipalities (8 cities and 31 towns) within 5 counties. Town clerks hold land records (called 'land evidence'), vital records, and probate records. The Rhode Island Judicial Archives (Pawtucket) holds county-level Superior Court records. Rhode Island was a major center of the colonial slave trade (especially Newport merchants) despite its small size. The state passed gradual emancipation in 1784, and slavery was effectively ended by 1842. Rhode Island's Industrial Revolution heritage (Slater Mill, 1793) means extensive mill and labor records exist from the late 18th century forward.

core_repositories.description: The official state repository for Rhode Island government records. Holds colonial records (General Assembly acts from 1646), state vital records (copies from 1853), military records, naturalization records, state census records (1865-1935), court records, and legislative petitions. The archives maintains the Rhode Island colony and state records that are essential for pre-town-level research.

Major manuscript repository with extensive collections of family papers, business records (including textile mill records), church records, photographs, and organizational archives. The RIHS Library holds published town vital records, town histories, genealogical periodicals, and the Rhode Island Genealogical Register. Essential for Rhode Island genealogical research beyond town-level records.

NEHGS has published the Rhode Island Vital Records series (James N. Arnold's compilation of early vital records to 1850) and maintains extensive databases of New England genealogical records. AmericanAncestors.org provides online access to indexed Rhode Island vital records, church records, and cemetery transcriptions. The Rhode Island Genealogical Register is a key NEHGS publication.

Archives of the city of Providence from its founding by Roger Williams in 1636. Holds the earliest continuous municipal records in Rhode Island including town meeting records, land evidence, tax records, and vital records. The 'Early Records of the Town of Providence' (published multi-volume set) covers 1636-1750 and is available at major research libraries.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including microfilmed town clerk records (vital records, land evidence, probate), state vital records, cemetery records, church records, and military records. FamilySearch's microfilm partnership with RI town clerks provides digitized records for most towns from their founding dates.

Comprehensive research guide covering RI record types, town-level record availability, county information, immigration records, and research strategies. Includes links to digitized records and town-specific research pages.

newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Rhode Island newspapers from the 1700s forward. Includes the Providence Gazette (1762+), Newport Mercury (1758+, one of America's oldest newspapers), and various town weeklies. Obituaries, legal notices, and estate advertisements are valuable genealogical sources.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Rhode Island Newspaper Project / Providence Public Library
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: The RI Newspaper Project cataloged and microfilmed Rhode Island newspapers. Providence Public Library holds an extensive microfilm collection of RI newspapers. Also accessible through Interlibrary Loan. Useful for locating obituaries, probate notices, and community news not found in official records.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Rhode Island Historical Society / Newport Historical Society / John Carter Brown Library
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Rhode Island (especially Newport and Providence) was a major center of the transatlantic slave trade. Records include ship manifests, merchant account books (DeWolf, Brown, Lopez, and Malbone families), customs house records, and manumission documents. The John Carter Brown Library at Brown University holds slave trade records. The Brown University Steering Committee on Slavery and Justice (2006) identified key archival sources. Gradual emancipation act of 1784 generated manumission and 'free negro' records at town clerk offices. Estate inventories and wills listing enslaved persons are found in town probate records, especially in Newport and Washington (Narragansett Planter) counties.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Rhode Island State Archives / Rhode Island Historical Society
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Rhode Island emancipation was gradual (1784 act, fully abolished by 1842). Records include: town vital records noting race, church records of Black congregations (Congdon Street Baptist Church, Providence, from 1820s), African Union Meeting House records, census records identifying free people of color, and military records of Black Rhode Islanders (First Rhode Island Regiment, Revolutionary War — one of the first Black regiments in US history).

military_records.source_name: Rhode Island State Archives — Military Records
military_records.description: State military records including colonial militia rolls, Revolutionary War records (RI was first colony to renounce allegiance to Britain, May 4, 1776), War of 1812 records, Civil War enlistment records and regimental histories, and WWI/WWII service records. The First Rhode Island Regiment (1778) was one of the first Black military units in American history. Colonial muster rolls from King Philip's War (1675-76) and French and Indian War.

military_records.source_name: NARA — Rhode Island Federal Military Records
military_records.description: Federal military records for Rhode Island residents: Revolutionary War pension files, Civil War compiled service records, WWI and WWII draft registration cards. NARA Northeast Region (Waltham, MA) holds regional federal records. Bounty land warrants and pension applications available through Fold3 and FamilySearch.

land_and_property.source_name: Rhode Island Town Land Evidence Records
land_and_property.description: In Rhode Island, land records (called 'land evidence') are held by individual town clerks, not at the county level. Each of RI's 39 municipalities maintains its own land evidence books from its founding date. The earliest records date to 1636 (Providence). FamilySearch has microfilmed many town land evidence records. The 'Early Records of the Town of Providence' (21 volumes, published 1892-1915) cover 1636-1750.

Colony-level land transactions, Indian land purchases, and proprietary records at the Rhode Island State Archives. Includes records of the original land grants and divisions in each town, boundary disputes, and the records of Rhode Island's General Assembly relating to land. Published in the 'Records of the Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations' (10 volumes, Bartlett, 1856-1865).

vital_records.source_name: Rhode Island Department of Health — Vital Records
vital_records.description: State vital records office holds copies of births, deaths, and marriages from 1853 to present. For records before 1853, researchers must consult individual town clerk offices. Historical vital records (100+ years for births, 50+ years for deaths/marriages) may be available through RI State Archives. Rhode Island town vital records are among the earliest in the United States — Providence recorded births and deaths from 1636.

vital_records.source_name: James N. Arnold — Rhode Island Vital Records to 1850
vital_records.description: Arnold's 'Vital Record of Rhode Island 1636-1850' (21 volumes, published 1891-1912) compiled vital records from town clerks, church records, and newspapers across Rhode Island. Organized by town and source type. Indexed and searchable on AmericanAncestors.org (NEHGS). This is the single most important compiled source for colonial and early RI vital records.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Rhode Island Church Records — RIHS and FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Rhode Island's founding principle of religious liberty produced exceptional denominational diversity and rich church records from the 1630s. Key collections: Baptist records (First Baptist Church of America, Providence, 1638); Quaker meeting records (extensive for Newport, Portsmouth, South Kingstown — New England Yearly Meeting records at RIHS); Jewish records (Touro Synagogue, Newport, 1763); Congregational, Episcopal, and Catholic records. RIHS holds many deposited church records. FamilySearch has microfilmed significant church record collections.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Find A Grave — Rhode Island Cemeteries
church_and_cemetery.description: Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Rhode Island. Coverage is generally good for this small state. Colonial-era burying grounds in Newport, Providence, and other early towns have been extensively photographed and transcribed. The Rhode Island Historical Cemeteries database (maintained by RIHS) provides a comprehensive inventory of cemetery locations statewide.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes Rhode Island Roots (quarterly journal) and maintains research guides, surname databases, and cemetery transcription projects. Offers research assistance and workshops for RI genealogical research.
Volunteer-contributed county and town-level genealogy resources including cemetery transcriptions, vital record extracts, town histories, and research guides for all Rhode Island municipalities.

Major local historical society with extensive manuscript collections, maritime records, merchant papers, and colonial-era documents. The NHS Library holds unique materials for Newport County genealogy including Touro Synagogue records, Quaker meeting records, shipping logs, and family papers spanning the colonial through modern periods.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Rhode Island has no extinct counties. The state's 5 counties (Providence, Newport, Washington, Kent, Bristol) have remained unchanged since Kent County was created in 1750. Washington County was originally named Kings County (created 1729) and renamed in 1781 during the Revolutionary War. Bristol County was transferred from Massachusetts to Rhode Island in 1747. Newport and Providence counties were created in 1703 as the original two counties of the colony. Prior to 1703, the colony had no county divisions — records from before that date are found in town records and colonial government records at the Rhode Island State Archives.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Rhode Island has no burned counties. The state's records have survived remarkably well, in part because counties have no courthouses — records are maintained at the town level across 39 municipalities, reducing the risk of catastrophic single-point-of-failure losses. Town halls have occasionally suffered fires (e.g., minor record gaps in some towns), but no Rhode Island jurisdiction has experienced the wholesale record destruction seen in 'burned county' states. The Rhode Island State Archives holds state-level copies of vital records from 1853 forward, providing an additional safeguard.
